questions.stem: A 19-year-old man presents with bilateral gynecomastia and small, firm testes (volume <4 mL). He is tall (188 cm) with a high-arched palate and long limbs. He has sparse facial and body hair and reports low libido. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
questions.explanation: The combination of tall stature, gynecomastia, small testes, and hypogonadism is classic for Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY). Kallmann syndrome would include anosmia; Marfan syndrome presents with lens dislocation and aortic dilation; Noonan syndrome features short stature and webbed neck; constitutional delay resolves with normal puberty.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy is brought to clinic due to tall stature and poor school performance. He has long limbs and a narrow face. Testes are descended but small (1 mL bilaterally). He has no other dysmorphic features. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome can present prepubertally with tall stature, learning difficulties, and small testes. Marfan syndrome includes lens dislocation and aortic root dilation; homocystinuria has lens dislocation and thrombosis risk; Sotos syndrome has macrocephaly and advanced bone age; Fragile X syndrome has characteristic facial features (prominent jaw, large ears).

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is admitted with acute pulmonary embolism. He has a history of unexplained venous thromboembolism. On examination, he is tall (190 cm) with scant body hair and testes measuring 3 mL each. What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome is associated with a hypercoagulable state and increased risk of venous thromboembolism. The physical findings of tall stature and small testes point to Klinefelter. Marfan syndrome predisposes to aortic dissection, not PE; homocystinuria would cause lens dislocation and intellectual disability; antiphospholipid syndrome causes recurrent thrombosis but no hypogonadism; sickle cell disease has a different phenotype.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is found to have a breast lump that is confirmed as invasive ductal carcinoma. He is tall with small testes and gynaecomastia. He has no family history of breast cancer. What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome confers a 20-50 fold increased risk of male breast cancer. The presence of tall stature and small testes before the cancer suggests the underlying diagnosis. Idiopathic male breast cancer is less likely given the phenotypic features; spironolactone, prolactinoma, and cirrhosis cause gynaecomastia but not a significantly elevated breast cancer risk.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old male presents with delayed puberty (Tanner stage G1) and a history of bilateral undescended testes requiring orchidopexy at age 5. He is tall (180 cm) and has mild learning difficulties. Testes are firm and 3 mL each. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
questions.explanation: Mosaic Klinefelter (47,XXY/46,XY) can present with milder features: delayed puberty, mild learning difficulties, and a history of undescended testes. Constitutional delay typically has normal growth and eventual puberty; Kallmann syndrome includes anosmia; Noonan syndrome has short stature; 46,XX male syndrome is rare and often presents similarly to Klinefelter but without the 47,XXY karyotype.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is referred with infertility. On examination, he has small, firm testes, bilateral gynecomastia, and a tall, slim build. What is the single most appropriate initial investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Karyotype demonstrating 47,XXY is the gold standard for diagnosing Klinefelter syndrome. Hormone levels and semen analysis may be abnormal but are not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with bilateral gynecomastia. Investigations show low serum testosterone (8 nmol/L, normal 10-30), elevated FSH (15 IU/L, normal 1-7), and elevated LH (12 IU/L, normal 1-8). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The hormone profile shows hypergonadotropic hypogonadism. In a young man with gynecomastia, Klinefelter syndrome is the most common cause. Other options do not fit the clinical and biochemical picture.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man undergoing infertility investigations has a karyotype reported as 47,XXY. Which of the following is the most likely clinical diagnosis?
questions.explanation: 47,XXY is the classic karyotype in Klinefelter syndrome. Noonan syndrome is associated with normal karyotype, Turner syndrome is 45,X, Down syndrome is trisomy 21, and Fragile X is a single-gene disorder.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome is found to have abnormal blood tests. Which of the following hormone profiles is most characteristic of this condition?
options: Normal testosterone, high FSH, high LH
Low testosterone, high FSH, normal LH
Low testosterone, low FSH, low LH
High testosterone, high FSH, high LH
Low testosterone, high FSH, high LH
questions.explanation: Primary testicular failure leads to hypergonadotropic hypogonadism: low testosterone with elevated FSH and LH due to loss of negative feedback. This pattern is characteristic of Klinefelter syndrome.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy is evaluated for tall stature and learning difficulties. His testes are small and prepubertal. Which of the following would you expect to find on routine blood tests for suspected Klinefelter syndrome?
questions.explanation: Before puberty, the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis is quiescent, so gonadotropins and testosterone are in the normal prepubertal range. Elevated levels only appear after pubertal onset; thus, a normal profile does not exclude Klinefelter syndrome.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with low libido, fatigue, and bilateral gynaecomastia. Examination reveals tall stature, small firm testes, and sparse body hair. Serum testosterone is 6 nmol/L (low), LH 15 IU/L (elevated), FSH 25 IU/L (elevated). Klinefelter syndrome is confirmed on karyotype (47,XXY). What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) 1000 IU twice weekly
questions.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome causes hypergonadotropic hypogonadism. Testosterone replacement is the cornerstone of therapy to improve libido, bone density, and muscle mass. hCG, clomiphene, and anastrozole are not effective in this setting because the defect is primary testicular failure, not hypothalamic-pituitary axis dysfunction.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) has never received testosterone replacement. He presents with a low-trauma hip fracture. DEXA scan confirms osteoporosis (T-score -3.0). What is the most appropriate definitive therapy for the underlying endocrine condition?
questions.explanation: Osteoporosis in Klinefelter syndrome is secondary to hypogonadism. Testosterone replacement is the first-line therapy to improve bone mineral density. Bisphosphonates or denosumab may be added if osteoporosis persists despite testosterone, but the primary treatment is testosterone replacement.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) and his wife wish to conceive. He has normal serum testosterone (14 nmol/L) and gonadotropins, but semen analysis reveals azoospermia. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Recommend intrauterine insemination with donor sperm
Start testosterone gel to improve spermatogenesis
questions.explanation: In Klinefelter syndrome, spermatogenesis is impaired due to testicular fibrosis. Focal areas of spermatogenesis may be present, and micro-TESE can retrieve sperm for ICSI. Testosterone therapy suppresses spermatogenesis and would be detrimental. Clomiphene or hCG are ineffective for primary testicular failure.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome has been on testosterone gel 50 mg daily for 5 years. He now presents with tender, bilateral breast enlargement. Examination confirms gynaecomastia (grade 2). Serum oestradiol is mildly elevated, and prolactin is normal. What is the most appropriate medical therapy?
options: Switch to testosterone undecanoate injection 1000 mg every 12 weeks
Increase testosterone gel dose to 100 mg daily
questions.explanation: Gynaecomastia in Klinefelter syndrome is driven by oestrogen excess relative to testosterone. An aromatase inhibitor (e.g., anastrozole) reduces peripheral conversion of androgens to oestrogens, providing targeted medical therapy. Tamoxifen is also used but anastrozole is first-line; surgical referral is reserved for persistent cases. Increasing testosterone may worsen gynaecomastia by providing more substrate for aromatisation.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old obese man (BMI 38 kg/m²) with Klinefelter syndrome is newly diagnosed with severe obstructive sleep apnoea (AHI 45 events/hour) on polysomnography. He has symptomatic hypogonadism (testosterone 5 nmol/L, LH 18 IU/L). Which therapy is CONTRAINDICATED as first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Testosterone therapy is contraindicated in untreated severe obstructive sleep apnoea because it can exacerbate the condition by increasing chemosensitivity and altering respiratory drive. CPAP and weight loss are recommended first; testosterone may be considered once OSA is treated. Anastrozole and clomiphene are not first-line therapies for hypogonadism in Klinefelter and would not be contraindicated for OSA.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is referred to the medical take with bilateral gynaecomastia and small, firm testes. He is otherwise well with no other symptoms. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Refer to breast surgery for gynaecomastia
questions.explanation: In a young man with suggestive features, karyotype confirms Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) before any treatment. Starting testosterone without diagnosis is inappropriate. Gynaecomastia is managed after diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known Klinefelter syndrome (diagnosed 5 years ago) presents with progressive fatigue, low libido, and reduced muscle strength. Serum testosterone is 6 nmol/L (12–30). What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Refer to endocrinology for further investigation
questions.explanation: Symptomatic hypogonadism with low testosterone warrants testosterone replacement (NICE/BNF). Referral is not needed before starting treatment. Other options are secondary or not first-line.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome (known cryptorchidism in childhood) presents to ED with acute severe left testicular pain, nausea, and vomiting. The left testis is swollen, tender, and high-riding. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Give analgesia and observe for 2 hours
questions.explanation: High suspicion of testicular torsion (cryptorchidism is a risk factor) requires emergency surgical exploration to salvage the testis. Ultrasound delays definitive management. Antibiotics are for infection, unlikely here.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome (never on testosterone) complains of gradual onset lower back pain and has lost 3 cm in height. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Check serum calcium, vitamin D, and PTH
questions.explanation: Height loss and back pain suggest vertebral fracture due to osteoporosis (common in untreated Klinefelter). DXA scan is the initial diagnostic test. Treatment decisions (bisphosphonates, testosterone) follow results. Calcium/vitamin D should be checked but DXA is the priority.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old university student with known Klinefelter syndrome (diagnosed at puberty, not on testosterone) is well and asymptomatic. He asks about his chances of having children. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Start testosterone replacement to improve fertility
Reassure that he is likely fertile
Discuss sperm banking before any treatment
questions.explanation: Semen analysis is the first step to assess fertility. Most patients are azoospermic, but if sperm are present, cryopreservation can be offered. Testosterone suppresses spermatogenesis. Referral follows results.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old male with tall stature, small testes, and gynaecomastia is found to have 47,XXY karyotype. As part of the diagnostic workup, a chest X-ray reveals an anterior mediastinal mass. Which of the following is the most serious differential diagnosis to exclude in this patient?
questions.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome (KS) is strongly associated with mediastinal germ cell tumours, particularly non-seminomas, which can be aggressive and life-threatening. Thymoma is less associated with KS and is not a germ cell tumour.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome on testosterone replacement therapy presents to the emergency department with severe headache, blurred vision, and drowsiness. Blood pressure is 170/100 mmHg. Fundoscopy reveals papilloedema. Full blood count shows haematocrit 0.55. Which single finding most directly indicates that this is an emergency requiring immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: A haematocrit >0.54 is the threshold for withholding testosterone therapy due to increased risk of stroke from polycythaemia. This finding directly indicates the need for urgent intervention, whereas papilloedema and hypertension are consequences.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old man with known Klinefelter syndrome presents with acute pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, and dyspnoea. He is not on any medications. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. Which of the following is the most likely life-threatening complication of his condition?
questions.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome is a risk factor for venous thromboembolism even without testosterone therapy. The pleuritic chest pain and haemoptysis are classic for pulmonary embolism, which is a life-threatening emergency.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome presents with a palpable lump in his left breast. On examination, the lump is firm, irregular, and nontender, with overlying skin dimpling. Which of the following is the most important diagnosis to suspect and exclude?
questions.explanation: Males with Klinefelter syndrome have a 20-50 times increased risk of breast cancer. Although gynaecomastia is common, the irregular, firm nature and skin dimpling suggest malignancy, which must be excluded urgently.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with Klinefelter syndrome who has not received testosterone therapy for several years presents with acute onset of severe lower back pain after minor strain. He is unable to bear weight and has reduced sensation in his legs. Which clinical finding most strongly indicates an emergency requiring urgent spinal imaging?
questions.explanation: Reduced sensation suggests possible spinal cord compression from a vertebral fracture due to osteoporosis, a known complication of untreated KS. This is a neurological emergency requiring immediate MRI. Severe back pain alone is common and not specific.
